introduction: An operating system is a busy hive of activity, with hundreds of programs running simultaneously in the background. In Linux, these running programs are called 'processes'. To manage a system effectively, DevOps engineers must be able to monitor CPU/memory usage, background or suspend tasks, and terminate processes that are frozen or consuming too many resources.

Furthermore, modern applications do not run in isolation. They communicate over networks, bind to TCP/UDP ports, listen for HTTP requests, and resolve domains using DNS. Whether you are deploying a microservice in Docker or debugging connection failures between a web app and a database, you must know how to inspect network interfaces, check open ports, audit active sockets, and verify connectivity parameters.

This module bridges processes management and networks configurations: managing processes using ps, top, and kill; utilizing jobs background controls; checking networking adapter bindings using ip address; auditing listening ports using ss; resolving DNS lookups via dig; and monitoring services status using systemctl.
what_you_will_learn: Monitoring system processes utilizing ps and top metrics tables.
Spawning background processes and managing jobs control with fg and jobs.
Terminating unresponsive threads using kill signals.
Inspecting local network cards interfaces and gateway routes.
Auditing TCP server listening socket ports using ss.
Testing server connections and domain routing via ping, curl, and dig.
overview.prerequisites: Completion of Bash Scripting Fundamentals course.
learning_outcomes: Monitor, stop, background, and foreground system tasks safely.
Diagnose local networks configuration adapter details and route tables.
Identify socket leaks and map listening ports to PIDs.
Verify DNS record registrations and retrieve headers via cURL.

theory.title: Demystifying Processes: Your System's Worker Ants
theory.definition: A 'Process' is just a program in action. Every time you run a command or launch an app, Linux spins up a process and gives it a unique Process ID (PID).
theory.explanation:
Think of your computer as a busy restaurant. The programs (like Python, Nginx, or Bash) are the recipes. But a recipe on a piece of paper doesn't feed anyone. When the chef actually starts cooking the recipe, it becomes a Process!

To keep track of everything, the restaurant manager assigns a ticket number to every order. In Linux, this ticket number is called a Process ID (PID).

Here are the commands you use to manage them:
- `ps aux`: Show a snapshot of every single worker ant (process) running on the system.
- `top` (or `htop`): A live dashboard showing which processes are eating up the CPU and memory (like Task Manager on Windows).
- `kill [PID]`: Stop a process. You can send a polite request to shut down (`kill -15 [PID]`) or, if it is completely frozen, force-terminate it instantly (`kill -9 [PID]`).
theory.why_exists: To let you monitor system health, locate resource hogs (like memory leaks), and shut down frozen applications safely.
theory.where_used: Debugging server performance issues, restarting crashed web services, and managing system resources.
theory.real_world_example: You deploy a Node.js app but it freezes. You run `ps aux | grep node` to find its PID (e.g. 2405), and run `kill -9 2405` to terminate it instantly.
theory.best_practices: Always try to shut down a process politely using SIGTERM (15) first. Only use SIGKILL (9) as a last resort, since it can corrupt database files if the process was halfway through writing data.
theory.common_mistakes: Blindly copy-pasting a PID from a Google tutorial. PIDs are completely random and change every time a program restarts!

theory.title: Sockets & Ports: The Mailboxes of Your Server
theory.definition: A 'Port' is a virtual slot on your network interface (labeled 1 to 65535) that helps incoming internet traffic find the correct program.
theory.explanation:
Imagine your server is a massive apartment building with the address `192.168.1.50`. If mail just gets sent to the building, how does the mailman know which resident it belongs to? They need an apartment number!

In networking, this apartment number is called a Port.
- Web servers (like HTTP) live in apartment 80 or 443.
- Databases (like PostgreSQL) live in apartment 5432.
- Secure shell access (SSH) lives in apartment 22.

To see which programs are listening in their apartments right now, you run:
`ss -lntp`
- `-l`: Show only listening slots.
- `-n`: Show numbers instead of names.
- `-t`: Show TCP sockets.
- `-p`: Show the actual PID/program name listening (like nginx or postgres).

If you try to launch a web server on port 80 but another program is already in that apartment, Linux will throw an 'Address already in use' error!
theory.why_exists: Enables a single server to handle multiple different internet services (web pages, databases, file transfers) at the exact same time.
theory.where_used: Configuring network firewalls, setting up Docker container mappings, and troubleshooting connection timeouts.
theory.real_world_example: Checking `ss -lntp` to confirm your backend API is successfully listening on port 8000 and ready to receive client requests.
theory.best_practices: For security, make sure database ports (like 5432) are configured to only listen locally (127.0.0.1) so they are not exposed to the public internet.
theory.common_mistakes: Forgetting that port numbers below 1024 are privileged. Regular users cannot start programs on port 80 or 443 without administrative permissions (using sudo).

interactive_examples.explanation: Executes the sleep utility in the background, returning a job ID [1] and PID. Keeps the prompt active.
interactive_examples.common_mistakes: Forgetting the space before the ampersand symbol.
interactive_examples.tips: Inspect background tasks anytime using the `jobs` command.

interactive_examples.explanation: Queries the kernel socket state table, listing TCP ports currently listening for connections.
interactive_examples.common_mistakes: Typing uppercase flags instead of lowercase -lnt.
interactive_examples.tips: Pass -p flag (requires root permissions) to output the PID of the process bound to the port.

lessons.title: 1. Terminal Processes (ps)
lessons.definition: Processes are active running instances of programs in Linux.
lessons.explanation: The ps command lists processes running in your current terminal session.
lessons.instruction: Print the processes running in this terminal session.

lessons.title: 2. System Processes (ps aux)
lessons.definition: ps aux prints all running processes across the whole system.
lessons.explanation: a (all users), u (detailed fields), x (processes not attached to terminals).
lessons.instruction: Expose details of all running processes in the system.

lessons.title: 3. Real-time Monitoring (top)
lessons.definition: top displays a real-time, dynamic view of active running processes.
lessons.explanation: Outputs CPU cycles, RAM consumptions, uptime details, and active threads.
lessons.instruction: Query process dynamic parameters using top.

lessons.title: 4. Terminate Processes (kill)
lessons.definition: The kill command sends signals to terminate running processes.
lessons.explanation: Requires a target Process ID (PID). SIGKILL (-9) forces immediate shutdown.
lessons.instruction: Terminate a process with PID 9999 (use simulation standard return).

lessons.title: 5. Background Jobs (sleep &)
lessons.definition: The ampersand (&) executes a command in the background.
lessons.explanation: Keeps the terminal responsive for more commands while it executes.
lessons.instruction: Run a sleep command for 100 seconds in the background.

lessons.title: 6. Job Controller (jobs)
lessons.definition: The jobs command lists background processes spawned by this shell.
lessons.explanation: Shows status (running, stopped) and job number identifiers.

lessons.title: 7. Bring to Foreground (fg)
lessons.definition: fg moves background jobs to the foreground, letting them take terminal focus.
lessons.explanation: Takes a job identifier parameter, for example, fg %1.
lessons.instruction: Bring background job 1 back to the foreground.

lessons.title: 8. Network Interfaces (ip a)
lessons.definition: ip address (ip a) displays local network adapters and IP configurations.
lessons.explanation: Used to verify local adapter status, loopbacks, and network parameters.

lessons.title: 9. Gateway Routes (ip route)
lessons.definition: ip route outputs network routing lookup tables.
lessons.explanation: Exposes destination gateways, interfaces, and gateway routing parameters.

lessons.title: 10. Connectivity Ping
lessons.definition: ping sends ICMP ECHO_REQUEST packets to verify connection status.
lessons.explanation: Validates if a remote host is alive and responding.

lessons.title: 11. Active Sockets (ss)
lessons.definition: ss dumps socket statistics, replacing the older netstat tool.
lessons.explanation: Shows active listening TCP/UDP ports, matching connection routes.

lessons.title: 12. DNS Lookup (dig)
lessons.definition: dig performs DNS record resolution lookups.
lessons.explanation: Queries name servers for A, MX, CNAME, and TXT mapping parameters.
lessons.instruction: Query DNS record details for google.com.

lessons.title: 13. HTTP Requests (curl)
lessons.definition: curl transfers data from or to a server using supported protocols.
lessons.explanation: Commonly used to test API services or download configurations.
lessons.instruction: Fetch headers from an endpoint using curl.

lessons.title: 14. Systemd Services
lessons.definition: systemctl manages systemd services status and boot processes.
lessons.explanation: Can inspect, launch, reload, disable, or stop daemon services.
lessons.instruction: Check the status of the nginx service.

lessons.title: 15. Restarting Daemons
lessons.definition: systemctl restart stops and immediately starts a system service.
lessons.explanation: Commonly executed after updating configuration files to reload parameters.
lessons.instruction: Restart the nginx service to reload configurations.

exercises.title: Background daemon checks
exercises.problem: Spawn a long-sleeping background process, find its PID using ps, and kill it with SIGKILL.
exercises.hint: Run `sleep 999 &` followed by `ps aux | grep sleep` then `kill -9 [PID]`.
exercises.objective: Practice background execution and target kill workflows.

exercises.title: Gateway interfaces audits
exercises.problem: Identify your active gateway route interfaces mapping.
exercises.expected_result: Outputs the default route interface info.

exercises.title: Audit Nginx ports
exercises.problem: Verify if the local web server is listening on port 80.

exercises.title: DNS records trace
exercises.problem: Resolve domain names IP registers for a custom nameserver.
exercises.expected_result: DNS answer section lists IP mapping details.

exercises.title: Curl API testing
exercises.problem: Test Nginx server configuration by fetching raw page headers.

exercises.title: Auto service reload
exercises.problem: Inspect the nginx systemd configuration files status and perform restarts.
exercises.hint: Use `systemctl status nginx` followed by `systemctl restart nginx`.

quiz.question: Which command lists running processes in your current terminal session?
quiz.explanation: ps displays process snapshots attached to your active TTY screen.

incorrect_explanations.jobs: Lists shell background tasks only, not all session threads.
incorrect_explanations.ss: Displays network socket connections, not active system processes.

quiz.question: What is the process ID (PID) of the first process initialized by the Linux kernel (init/systemd)?
quiz.explanation: PID 1 is reserved for the initial system systemd/init task, parent of all other threads.

incorrect_explanations.0: PID 0 is reserved for scheduler/swapper, not a standard system task.
incorrect_explanations.100: PID 100 is assigned to standard children daemons.

quiz.question: Which signal number is sent by kill -9 to force termination?
quiz.explanation: Signal 9 tells the Linux kernel to immediately stop the process, bypassing normal cleanup.

incorrect_explanations.SIGTERM (15): Signal 15 requests graceful termination, allowing state saving.
incorrect_explanations.SIGINT (2): Interrupt signal sent by Ctrl+C in terminal sessions.
incorrect_explanations.SIGHUP (1): Hangup signal typically used to reload configurations.

quiz.question: How do you run a command in the background from your shell?
quiz.explanation: Appending an ampersand & executes the program as a background job task.

incorrect_explanations.Prefix bg: bg is a separate command that resumes stopped jobs in the background.
incorrect_explanations.Append %: % references job IDs inside foreground/kill commands.
incorrect_explanations.Prefix systemctl: systemctl manages daemon services, it does not launch random CLI tasks in background.

quiz.question: Which utility checks IP address configurations of local network interfaces?
quiz.explanation: ip address (abbreviated ip a) prints local interface adapters, configurations, and network states.

incorrect_explanations.ss: ss queries network sockets, not adapter hardware configurations.
incorrect_explanations.dig: dig performs external DNS routing queries.

quiz.question: Which command checks TCP server listening sockets?
quiz.explanation: ss with -l (listening) and -n (numeric) outputs TCP ports active on the machine.

incorrect_explanations.ping: Tests ICMP connectivity, does not show ports.

quiz.question: True or False: The ping utility validates if a specific port (like 80) is listening on a server.
quiz.explanation: ping utilizes ICMP packets that verify machine connectivity, not specific socket ports.

incorrect_explanations.True: To test specific ports, use curl, telnet, or netcat instead.

quiz.question: Which DNS query tool returns A record registrations?
quiz.explanation: dig (domain information groper) resolves DNS mappings and returns domain details.

quiz.question: Which systemctl command stops and immediately starts a service?
quiz.explanation: restart performs a stop action followed by a start action, resetting process parameters.

incorrect_explanations.reload: Asks the service process to re-read configs without stopping the main process.
incorrect_explanations.start: Launches a stopped service, has no effect if running.
incorrect_explanations.enable: Configures the service to launch automatically during system boots.

quiz.question: Scenario: A web app client is failing to connect to your service. You want to verify Nginx is actively listening. What command do you run?
quiz.explanation: ss -lntp checks all listening ports along with the process IDs and names associated with each socket.

incorrect_explanations.systemctl enable nginx: Enables boot start but does not report active port details.
incorrect_explanations.dig localhost: Performs DNS lookup on localhost, which does not check port bindings.

resources.summary: This module reviews processes lifecycle controls ps, top, kill, jobs foregrounding, network adapters configuration ip, gateway routes, active socket ports audits ss, and systemd services scripts systemctl.
resources.cheat_sheet:
ps aux - show all processes
top - real-time monitor
kill -9 [PID] - force kill
sleep 10 & - start background job
jobs - list background jobs
fg %[id] - foreground job
ip a - show network cards
ss -lntp - show open ports
dig [domain] - DNS lookup
curl -I [url] - fetch web headers
systemctl restart [service] - restart daemon

commands_table.name: kill
commands_table.description: Sends signal code to target PID context.

revision_notes: Process signal 15 (SIGTERM) allows graceful shutdown, signal 9 (SIGKILL) forces termination.
Use `ss -lntp` as root to map listening ports to active program names.
DNS lookups utilize the local resolver configs configured at `/etc/resolv.conf`.
beginner_mistakes: Killing correct program PIDs using incorrect signal contexts.
Confusing `systemctl enable` (run on startup boot) with `systemctl start` (runs service immediately).
best_practices: Background long-running migration scripts using `nohup` or screen/tmux environments.
Verify port availability with `ss` before setting host port configs.

interview_questions.question: How do you check memory metrics in real-time?
interview_questions.answer: Use top, htop, or free -m commands.

interview_questions.question: What does a LISTEN state represent on port 80?
interview_questions.answer: It indicates a web server is bound and ready to accept web connections.

additional_practice: Spawn a background script logging to file and inspect it using top.
Verify local HTTP configuration response code using cURL.

books.title: UNIX and Linux System Administration Handbook
books.description: The golden standard guide for Linux systems management.

documentation.title: systemd System and Service Manager Documentation
